name: Health and Nutrition
description: A comprehensive exploration of nutrition science covering macronutrients, micronutrients, hydration, dietary guidelines, sports nutrition, and global food systems for healthy adolescent lifestyles.

modules.title: Introduction to Nutrition
modules.objectives: Define nutrition and its importance to health, identify the six classes of nutrients, and understand how food choices affect overall wellness.
modules.readingNote: USDA MyPlate — What Is MyPlate? — for background reading on Introduction to Nutrition before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Six Classes of Nutrients Overview
modules.practiceNote: Use the MyPlate plan tool to assess your current dietary pattern
modules.assignment: Keep a 3-day food diary and analyze your intake using the MyPlate framework, identifying nutrients you consume adequately and areas for improvement. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Introduction to Nutrition module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Carbohydrates
modules.titleZh: 宏量营养素：碳水化合物
modules.objectives: Identify types of carbohydrates (simple vs. complex, fiber), understand their role as the body's primary energy source, and evaluate healthy carbohydrate food choices.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Carbohydrates
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Carbohydrates Explained — for a visual explanation of Macronutrients: Carbohydrates before guided practice
modules.practiceNote: Identify five high-quality carbohydrate sources and five poor-quality sources from your diet
modules.assignment: Create a report comparing simple and complex carbohydrates, including the glycemic index concept, fiber's role in health, and three food swaps for better carbohydrate choices. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Macronutrients: Carbohydrates module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Proteins
modules.objectives: Explain the structure and functions of protein, differentiate complete from incomplete proteins, and determine protein needs for different activity levels.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — The Nutrition Source: Protein
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Health and medicine lesson videos for this topic: Macronutrients: Proteins.
modules.practiceNote: Identify your estimated daily protein needs and plan a high-protein meal that includes a complete protein source
modules.assignment: Calculate your recommended daily protein intake based on body weight and activity level, and plan a full day of meals that meets this target with diverse protein sources. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Macronutrients: Proteins module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Fats
modules.objectives: Classify dietary fats (saturated, unsaturated, trans), understand their health implications, and identify food sources of healthy fats. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Fats and Cholesterol
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Health and medicine lesson videos for this topic: Macronutrients: Fats.
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a food label for fat content and classify fats found in the product
modules.assignment: Write a guide explaining the three main types of dietary fat, their health effects, food sources for each, and practical advice for choosing heart-healthy fats. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Macronutrients: Fats module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Micronutrients: Vitamins
modules.objectives: Identify water-soluble and fat-soluble vitamins, their functions, food sources, and consequences of deficiency or excess. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamins and Minerals
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Health and medicine lesson videos for this topic: Micronutrients: Vitamins.
modules.practiceNote: Choose two vitamins and research their functions, deficiency symptoms, and best food sources
modules.assignment: Create a vitamin reference chart for 8 key vitamins (4 fat-soluble, 4 water-soluble) including daily recommended amounts, deficiency symptoms, and top food sources. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Micronutrients: Vitamins module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Micronutrients: Minerals
modules.objectives: Identify key dietary minerals (calcium, iron, potassium, sodium, zinc), their roles in body function, deficiency conditions, and food sources. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Minerals
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Health and medicine lesson videos for this topic: Micronutrients: Minerals.
modules.practiceNote: Analyze your typical diet for calcium and iron intake adequacy
modules.assignment: Research three minerals important for adolescent health (calcium, iron, zinc), explaining their functions, recommended daily intake, and consequences of deficiency with dietary solutions. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Micronutrients: Minerals module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Water & Hydration
modules.objectives: Explain water's roles in the body, understand daily hydration requirements, and recognize signs of dehydration and overhydration. Students will connect concepts to safe practice, explain risks or benefits, and document evidence of understanding in the module assignment.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Water
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Dehydration: Signs and Effects on the Body
modules.practiceNote: Track your fluid intake for 3 days and compare to daily recommended amounts
modules.assignment: Write a hydration guide for teenagers that explains daily water needs, factors that increase hydration needs, signs of dehydration, and tips for staying hydrated. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Water & Hydration module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Energy Balance & Weight Management
modules.objectives: Explain the concept of energy balance (calories in vs. out), understand basal metabolic rate, and evaluate healthy approaches to weight management.
modules.readingNote: CDC — Balancing Calories — for background reading on Energy Balance & Weight Management before the quiz and assignment
modules.videoNote: OpenStax — Principles of Management open lesson resource for this topic: Energy Balance & Weight Management.
modules.practiceNote: Use CDC BMI calculator and healthy weight resources to assess personal weight status
modules.assignment: Research the concept of energy balance and write a one-page essay explaining why crash diets are ineffective and unsustainable compared to lifestyle-based approaches. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: notes, examples, evidence, calculations, sources, or analysis from the Energy Balance & Weight Management module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six classes of nutrients?
quizQuestions.answer: Enzymes
quizQuestions.explanation: The six classes of nutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water. Enzymes are biological catalysts, not a nutrient class.

quizQuestions.question: Which nutrient class provides the most calories per gram?
quizQuestions.answer: Fat
quizQuestions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram, compared to 4 calories per gram for both carbohydrates and protein.

quizQuestions.question: Which option is not one of the six major classes of nutrients?
quizQuestions.answer: Enzymes
quizQuestions.explanation: The six major nutrient classes are carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Good nutrition is important for adolescents primarily because.
quizQuestions.answer: Good nutrition is important for adolescents because it supports rapid growth, brain development, energy, immune function, learning, athletic activity, and long-term healthy habits.
quizQuestions.explanation: Adolescence is a major growth period, so nutrition affects both daily performance and long-term health patterns.

quizQuestions.question: Which food choice best represents a high-quality carbohydrate source for steady energy?
options: Oatmeal or whole-grain bread with fiber
Butter as the main energy source
quizQuestions.answer: Oatmeal or whole-grain bread with fiber
quizQuestions.explanation: High-quality carbohydrate sources provide energy along with fiber and nutrients, which support steadier blood sugar and longer-lasting fuel.

quizQuestions.question: Dietary fiber is best described as:
options: An indigestible carbohydrate that supports digestive health
quizQuestions.answer: An indigestible carbohydrate that supports digestive health
quizQuestions.explanation: Dietary fiber is a complex carbohydrate that humans cannot fully digest, supporting digestive health, satiety, and blood sugar regulation.

quizQuestions.question: The glycemic index measures:
options: The total carbohydrate content of a food
How quickly a food raises blood glucose levels
The amount of sugar in a product
quizQuestions.answer: How quickly a food raises blood glucose levels
quizQuestions.explanation: The glycemic index ranks foods by how quickly they raise blood sugar; high-GI foods cause rapid spikes, low-GI foods cause gradual rises.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary function of carbohydrates in the body?
options: Providing energy (especially for the brain and during exercise)
quizQuestions.answer: Providing energy (especially for the brain and during exercise)
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrates are the body's preferred energy source, especially for brain function and moderate-to-high intensity exercise.

quizQuestions.question: A complete protein contains:
options: Only essential amino acids from plants
All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
quizQuestions.answer: All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
quizQuestions.explanation: A complete protein provides all nine essential amino acids that the body cannot synthesize on its own.

quizQuestions.question: Which food source provides complete protein?
quizQuestions.answer: Eggs
quizQuestions.explanation: Eggs are a complete protein source containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate proportions.

quizQuestions.question: The recommended daily protein intake for active teenagers is approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: 0.8–1.2 g/kg body weight
quizQuestions.explanation: Active adolescents generally need 0.8–1.2 g/kg of body weight daily, with higher amounts for strength athletes.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Vegetarians can obtain all essential amino acids by.
quizQuestions.answer: Vegetarians can obtain all essential amino acids by eating a varied pattern of protein-rich foods, such as beans, lentils, grains, nuts, seeds, dairy, eggs, soy foods, or other complete proteins as appropriate.
quizQuestions.explanation: The key idea is variety across the diet. Students do not need to rely on one food, but they should include enough high-quality protein sources to cover essential amino acids.

quizQuestions.question: Saturated fats are primarily found in:
quizQuestions.answer: Butter, red meat, and cheese
quizQuestions.explanation: Saturated fats are abundant in animal products like butter, red meat, and full-fat dairy, and are linked to elevated LDL cholesterol.

quizQuestions.question: Trans fats are most harmful because they:
options: Raise LDL (bad) cholesterol and lower HDL (good) cholesterol simultaneously
quizQuestions.answer: Raise LDL (bad) cholesterol and lower HDL (good) cholesterol simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: Trans fats raise LDL and lower HDL cholesterol, significantly increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease.

quizQuestions.question: Omega-3 fatty acids are especially beneficial for:
options: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
quizQuestions.answer: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
quizQuestions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA) reduce inflammation, support cardiovascular health, and promote brain development and function.

quizQuestions.question: Which fat is considered the healthiest choice?
options: Trans fat from partially hydrogenated oils
Saturated fat from coconut oil in excess
Any fat found in fast food
quizQuestions.answer: Monounsaturated fat from olive oil
quizQuestions.explanation: Monounsaturated fats, as found in olive oil, avocados, and nuts, are associated with improved heart health and reduced inflammation.

quizQuestions.question: Which vitamins are fat-soluble?
options: Vitamins A, D, E, and K
quizQuestions.answer: Vitamins A, D, E, and K
quizQuestions.explanation: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in body fat and require dietary fat for absorption; B vitamins and C are water-soluble.

quizQuestions.question: Vitamin C deficiency causes which disease?
quizQuestions.answer: Scurvy
quizQuestions.explanation: Scurvy results from vitamin C deficiency, causing collagen breakdown, bleeding gums, poor wound healing, and fatigue.

quizQuestions.question: Which set contains the fat-soluble vitamins?
options: All vitamins dissolve only in water
quizQuestions.answer: A, D, E, and K
quizQuestions.explanation: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Micronutrients: Vitamins.

quizQuestions.question: Vitamin C deficiency is associated with which disease?
quizQuestions.answer: Scurvy
quizQuestions.explanation: Severe vitamin C deficiency can lead to scurvy; rickets is associated with vitamin D deficiency.

quizQuestions.question: Calcium is most critical for which body function?
options: Bone and teeth formation and strength
quizQuestions.answer: Bone and teeth formation and strength
quizQuestions.explanation: Calcium is the primary mineral in bones and teeth, essential for their formation, density, and structural strength.

quizQuestions.question: Iron deficiency leads to which condition?
quizQuestions.answer: Iron-deficiency anemia
quizQuestions.explanation: Iron deficiency reduces hemoglobin production, impairing oxygen delivery and causing iron-deficiency anemia.

quizQuestions.question: Which food is the richest dietary source of calcium?
options: Dairy products like milk and yogurt
quizQuestions.answer: Dairy products like milk and yogurt
quizQuestions.explanation: Dairy products (milk, yogurt, cheese) are among the richest and most bioavailable sources of dietary calcium.

quizQuestions.question: Calcium is especially important for which body function?
options: Bone and teeth formation and strength
quizQuestions.answer: Bone and teeth formation and strength
quizQuestions.explanation: Calcium supports bone and tooth structure, along with other body functions.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: Excessive sodium intake is associated with.
quizQuestions.answer: Excessive sodium intake is associated with higher blood pressure and increased long-term risk of heart disease and stroke.
quizQuestions.explanation: Sodium affects fluid balance and blood pressure, so the strongest answer connects excess intake to cardiovascular risk rather than only to thirst or taste.

quizQuestions.question: Water makes up approximately what percentage of the human body?
quizQuestions.explanation: Water makes up approximately 60–70% of the adult body, with higher percentages in lean mass and lower in fat mass.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an early sign of dehydration?
quizQuestions.answer: Dark yellow urine and thirst
quizQuestions.explanation: Dark urine and thirst are early indicators of mild dehydration before significant performance or health impairment occurs.

quizQuestions.question: About what share of the human body is water by weight?
quizQuestions.explanation: A common educational estimate is that water makes up about 60-70% of body weight.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Which beverage is the best choice for everyday hydration?
quizQuestions.answer: Water is the best choice for everyday hydration because it hydrates without added sugar, caffeine, or unnecessary calories.
quizQuestions.explanation: Sports drinks may be useful in limited high-intensity or long-duration situations, but plain water is the default daily choice for most students.

quizQuestions.question: In Energy Balance & Weight Management, Energy balance means:
quizQuestions.answer: Calories consumed equal calories burned
quizQuestions.explanation: Energy balance occurs when caloric intake equals caloric expenditure, resulting in weight maintenance.

quizQuestions.question: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) refers to:
options: Calories burned at rest to maintain basic body functions
The rate at which food is digested
quizQuestions.answer: Calories burned at rest to maintain basic body functions
quizQuestions.explanation: BMR is the number of calories the body burns at rest to maintain basic physiological functions like breathing and circulation.

quizQuestions.question: A caloric deficit of approximately 500 calories per day leads to approximately how much weight loss per week?
quizQuestions.answer: 1 pound per week
quizQuestions.explanation: A 500-calorie daily deficit (3,500 calories per week) theoretically results in approximately 1 pound of fat loss per week.

quizQuestions.question: What does energy balance mean in basic nutrition?
options: Calories consumed roughly equal calories used
Calories consumed always exceed calories used
Exercise has no effect on energy use
quizQuestions.answer: Calories consumed roughly equal calories used
quizQuestions.explanation: Energy balance compares energy intake with energy expenditure over time. It connects the correct answer to Energy Balance & Weight Management and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Which of the six nutrient classes provides 9 calories per gram?
questions.answer: Fat
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram — more than twice the energy density of carbohydrates or protein (4 cal/g each).

questions.question: The glycemic index of a food indicates:
options: How rapidly it raises blood glucose levels
questions.answer: How rapidly it raises blood glucose levels
questions.explanation: The glycemic index measures how quickly a food raises blood sugar; high-GI foods cause rapid spikes.

questions.question: In Health and Nutrition midterm concepts, Complete proteins contain:
options: All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
questions.answer: All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
questions.explanation: Complete proteins supply all nine essential amino acids the body cannot make on its own.

questions.question: Why are trans fats considered especially harmful in nutrition education?
options: They can raise LDL cholesterol, lower HDL cholesterol, and increase heart disease risk
They are the main source of vitamin C
They are required for every meal
questions.answer: They can raise LDL cholesterol, lower HDL cholesterol, and increase heart disease risk
questions.explanation: Public health guidance generally recommends limiting trans fats because of their effect on blood cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.

questions.question: Which vitamin group is stored more readily in body fat than water-soluble vitamins?
questions.answer: A, D, E, and K
questions.explanation: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble, requiring dietary fat for absorption and stored in body fat.

questions.question: Iron deficiency primarily causes:
questions.answer: Iron-deficiency anemia
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency reduces hemoglobin production, resulting in iron-deficiency anemia with fatigue, pallor, and reduced oxygen transport.

questions.question: Which is an early sign of dehydration?
questions.answer: Early signs of dehydration can include thirst, dry mouth, darker urine, fatigue, headache, or reduced concentration.
questions.explanation: A strong response names observable early warning signs and connects them to the need to replace fluids before dehydration becomes more serious.

questions.question: Energy balance occurs when which relationship is roughly true?
options: Calories consumed roughly equal calories used
Calories consumed exceed calories used every day
questions.answer: Calories consumed roughly equal calories used
questions.explanation: Energy balance means intake and expenditure are approximately equal over time.

questions.question: The 2020–2025 Dietary Guidelines limit added sugars to less than:
questions.answer: 10% of daily calories
questions.explanation: The Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommend consuming less than 10% of daily calories from added sugars.

questions.question: Sports drinks are most appropriate for exercise lasting:
questions.answer: 60–90+ minutes at moderate-to-high intensity
questions.explanation: Sports drinks with electrolytes and carbohydrates become beneficial during sustained moderate-to-high intensity exercise lasting 60–90+ minutes.

questions.question: Omega-3 fatty acids are primarily known for supporting:
options: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
questions.answer: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
questions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) reduce inflammation and support cardiovascular health and brain function.

questions.question: Which description best identifies a complete protein food?
options: A food that provides all essential amino acids, such as eggs, dairy, meat, fish, soy, or quinoa
A food that contains only carbohydrates
A food with no amino acids
Any food that is low in calories
questions.answer: A food that provides all essential amino acids, such as eggs, dairy, meat, fish, soy, or quinoa
questions.explanation: Complete proteins contain all essential amino acids in meaningful amounts. This reinforces the Health and Nutrition midterm concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: Dietary fiber's primary health benefit is:
options: Supporting digestive health, satiety, and blood sugar regulation
questions.answer: Supporting digestive health, satiety, and blood sugar regulation
questions.explanation: Dietary fiber promotes healthy digestion, reduces cholesterol absorption, supports blood sugar control, and increases satiety.

questions.question: The recommended daily fluid intake for active teenagers is approximately:
questions.answer: For many older teens, a reasonable daily target is roughly 8 to 11 cups of total fluids, with additional water needed for heat, sweating, longer exercise, or larger body size.
questions.explanation: Fluid needs vary by age, body size, weather, diet, and activity level, so the safest model answer gives an approximate range and explains when intake should increase.

questions.question: Calcium is most important during adolescence for:
options: Building peak bone mass for lifelong bone health
questions.answer: Building peak bone mass for lifelong bone health
questions.explanation: Adolescence is the critical window for peak bone mass development; adequate calcium intake during this period reduces lifelong osteoporosis risk.

questions.question: Which MyPlate recommendation is commonly linked with chronic disease prevention?
options: Fill about half your plate with fruits and vegetables
Fill half your plate with grains only
Drink sugar-sweetened beverages with every meal
questions.answer: Fill about half your plate with fruits and vegetables
questions.explanation: MyPlate emphasizes fruits and vegetables as a major part of balanced eating patterns.

questions.question: Anorexia nervosa is primarily characterized by:
options: Severe food restriction and intense fear of weight gain
questions.answer: Severe food restriction and intense fear of weight gain
questions.explanation: Anorexia involves extreme caloric restriction, distorted body image, and intense fear of gaining weight.

questions.question: Older adults' caloric needs decrease with age primarily because:
options: Basal metabolic rate declines with reduced muscle mass
questions.answer: Basal metabolic rate declines with reduced muscle mass
questions.explanation: Age-related muscle loss (sarcopenia) and reduced physical activity lower overall energy requirements in older adults.

questions.question: Food waste in landfills contributes to climate change by producing:
questions.answer: Methane, a potent greenhouse gas
questions.explanation: Organic food decomposing in landfills produces methane, a greenhouse gas approximately 25 times more potent than CO2 over a century.

questions.question: The DASH eating plan was designed to help reduce what health risk factor?
questions.answer: High blood pressure
questions.explanation: DASH stands for Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension and is taught as a heart-health eating pattern.

questions.question: Which nutrient is uniquely produced by the body through sunlight exposure?
questions.answer: Vitamin D is the nutrient the body can produce when ultraviolet rays from sunlight reach the skin.
questions.explanation: Vitamin D can also come from foods or supplements, but this question focuses on the body's ability to synthesize vitamin D through sun exposure.

questions.question: Post-workout nutrition should emphasize which two nutrients?
questions.answer: Protein and carbohydrates
questions.explanation: Post-exercise recovery is optimized by consuming protein for muscle repair and carbohydrates for glycogen replenishment.

questions.question: Which statement best describes food insecurity?
options: Lack of consistent access to sufficient, affordable, nutritious food
questions.answer: Lack of consistent access to sufficient, affordable, nutritious food
questions.explanation: Food insecurity refers to the limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate and safe food.

questions.question: The percent Daily Value (%DV) on most U.S. food labels is based on what daily calorie reference?
questions.answer: 2,000 calories
questions.explanation: Food labels generally use a 2,000-calorie daily reference for percent Daily Value.

questions.question: Carbohydrate loading benefits athletes competing in events lasting:
questions.answer: 90+ minutes
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate loading maximizes muscle glycogen stores, delaying fatigue in endurance events lasting 90 minutes or more.

questions.question: Folate (folic acid) is especially important during pregnancy to prevent:
questions.answer: Neural tube defects
questions.explanation: Folate is essential for proper neural tube development in early pregnancy; deficiency is linked to spina bifida and anencephaly.

questions.question: Ingredients on a food label are listed in order of:
options: Weight from most to least abundant
questions.answer: Weight from most to least abundant
questions.explanation: Food ingredients are listed by weight from the most to least abundant ingredient in the product.

questions.question: Choosing locally grown, seasonal produce supports sustainability by:
questions.answer: Choosing locally grown, seasonal produce can support sustainability by reducing long-distance transportation and storage needs while supporting regional food systems.
questions.explanation: The strongest answer uses cautious wording because sustainability depends on farming and distribution practices, but local seasonal food can reduce some resource demands.

questions.question: Binge Eating Disorder differs from bulimia because BED:
options: Involves binges without compensatory purging behaviors
questions.answer: Involves binges without compensatory purging behaviors
questions.explanation: BED involves recurrent binge eating episodes without purging behaviors, distinguishing it from bulimia nervosa.

questions.question: The Mediterranean diet's heart health benefits are largely attributed to:
options: High saturated fat from red meat
High fruit, vegetable, whole grain, legume, and olive oil intake
High sugar and processed food consumption
questions.answer: High fruit, vegetable, whole grain, legume, and olive oil intake
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean diet's cardiovascular benefits come from its high intake of anti-inflammatory plant foods and heart-healthy fats like olive oil.

questions.question: Which macronutrient has the highest caloric density?
questions.answer: Fat (9 cal/g)
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram, the highest energy density of any macronutrient.

questions.question: Severe vitamin C deficiency can lead to which condition?
questions.answer: Scurvy
questions.explanation: Vitamin C deficiency is classically associated with scurvy. Students need to recognize why Scurvy matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Which dietary pattern has the smallest environmental carbon footprint?
questions.answer: Mostly plant-based foods
questions.explanation: Plant-based foods require significantly less land, water, and energy and produce fewer greenhouse gas emissions than animal products.

questions.question: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) represents:
options: Calories burned at rest to maintain vital body functions
Total daily energy needs including all activity
Caloric intake from a typical meal
questions.answer: Calories burned at rest to maintain vital body functions
questions.explanation: BMR is the minimum energy the body needs at complete rest to sustain vital functions like breathing, circulation, and thermoregulation.

questions.question: Sodium's excessive intake is most associated with increased risk of:
questions.answer: Excessive sodium intake is most associated with increased risk of high blood pressure, which can raise risk for heart disease and stroke.
questions.explanation: This answer identifies the direct health concern, high blood pressure, and connects it to broader cardiovascular risk.
